refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), includes both uplink and downlink paths, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with simulated remote tasks. Validate p95 latency across 1000+ control cycles. Establish baseline latency budget allocation across sensor acquisition, processing, transmission, and actuator response.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Acquire sensor data and prepare telemetry packets with minimal acquisition latency (target: <20 ms)
FR-NGHE-008.2: Transmit telemetry and tasking commands over network link with bounded transit latency (target: <80 ms p95 under agreed conditions)
FR-NGHE-008.3: Process remote operator commands and generate actuator control signals with minimal processing latency (target: <30 ms)
FR-NGHE-008.4: Monitor and report latency health; trigger safety alerts if latency exceeds safe thresholds

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement distributed real-time processing architecture with edge compute on vehicle, optimized network codec, and operator-side command buffering to minimize end-to-end latency
DP-B: Deploy dedicated low-latency network overlay (e.g., QoS-prioritized VPN or private network slice) with hardware-accelerated packet processing and deterministic scheduling
DP-C: Hybrid approach combining edge compute optimization (DP-A) with network QoS guarantees (DP-B) and latency monitoring/failover logic
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid approach combining edge compute optimization with network QoS guarantees and latency monitoring. This DP addresses both the deterministic latency requirement (via QoS and scheduling) and operational resilience (via monitoring and failover). It decouples sensor/actuator processing (vehicle-side) from network transport (infrastructure-side), enabling independent optimization and fault isolation.

potential_sources: Coupling between vehicle processing latency and network transport latency is managed via explicit latency budget allocation and monitoring.
Coupling between operator interface responsiveness and command processing is mitigated by command buffering and predictive control logic.
Coupling between safety alert generation and latency monitoring is managed via independent safety services.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C exhibits low coupling because it separates concerns: vehicle-side processing, network transport, and safety monitoring are independently optimizable. The hybrid approach allows graceful degradation if one component (e.g., QoS) is unavailable.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network infrastructure supports agreed QoS profiles (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss). Risk: If actual network conditions deviate, latency targets may not be met. Mitigation: Establish network SLA and continuous monitoring.

Assumption: Edge compute on vehicle can sustain real-time processing without thermal or power constraints. Risk: Vehicle compute may saturate under high sensor data rates. Mitigation: Implement adaptive sensor sampling and data compression.

Assumption: Operator-side command buffering does not introduce unacceptable control lag. Risk: Buffering may mask network latency spikes, degrading operator awareness. Mitigation: Provide latency feedback to operator and implement predictive control.
Assumption: Latency monitoring overhead is negligible (<5 ms). Risk: Monitoring itself may introduce latency. Mitigation: Use hardware timestamping and asynchronous monitoring.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) latency budget allocation across vehicle processing, network transport, and operator processing; (2) QoS mechanism (e.g., 5G network slicing, dedicated VPN, or private network); (3) edge compute architecture (e.g., real-time OS, deterministic scheduling); (4) latency monitoring and failover logic (e.g., trigger safety alert if p95 latency exceeds 150 ms for >N consecutive cycles).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns end-to-end latency optimization, including vehicle-side sensor acquisition and command processing, network transport coordination, and operator-side command buffering. It interfaces with Vehicle (for sensor/actuator access), Safety Services (for latency health monitoring and alerts), and Network Link (for transport).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs telemetry (sensor data, vehicle state, latency metrics) to operator console and Safety Services. Telemetry packets are time-stamped and prioritized for low-latency transmission.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (tasking) from remote operator console. Commands are buffered, validated, and forwarded to Vehicle for actuator control.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs SafetyAlert signals to Safety Services when latency health degrades (e.g., p95 latency exceeds threshold, packet loss exceeds limit, or network link fails).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link for bidirectional communication (telemetry uplink, tasking downlink). Coordinates with network infrastructure for QoS and latency monitoring.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. Assigning to NGHE Solution would diffuse latency responsibility across multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Safety, Network). Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle owns sensor acquisition and actuator control, which are components of end-to-end latency. However, Vehicle does not own network transport or operator-side processing. Assigning latency ownership to Vehicle alone would split responsibility and obscure network/operator contributions. Partial containment only.

Teleoperation is the natural owner of end-to-end latency optimization. It encompasses vehicle-side processing, network coordination, and operator-side buffering. Latency monitoring and failover logic are core to teleoperation. High containment, low diffusion. Recommended.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and alerts, not latency optimization. Assigning latency ownership to Safety Services would conflate safety monitoring with latency control. Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert signals from Teleoperation, not own latency. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of CR-NGHE-008 because it encapsulates the full end-to-end latency path: vehicle-side sensor/command processing, network transport, and operator-side buffering. Latency optimization is intrinsic to teleoperation functionality, not a cross-cutting concern. Teleoperation has clear interfaces to Vehicle (Telemetry, Tasking), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and Network Link (Network Link). Ownership is cohesive and non-diffuse.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become overloaded if it also owns other latency-sensitive functions (e.g., video streaming, sensor fusion). Mitigation: Clearly scope Teleoperation to command/control latency; delegate video/sensor fusion to separate modules.

Risk: Vehicle-side processing latency is partially owned by Vehicle module (sensor drivers, actuator control). Teleoperation must coordinate with Vehicle to ensure latency budget is met. Mitigation: Establish latency SLA between Teleoperation and Vehicle; include latency metrics in Vehicle interface contract.

Risk: Network Link is external infrastructure; Teleoperation cannot fully control network latency. Mitigation: Establish network SLA; implement latency monitoring and failover logic in Teleoperation to detect and respond to network degradation.

Risk: Operator-side latency (command processing, display refresh) is outside Teleoperation module scope if operator console is a separate system. Mitigation: Define operator-side latency budget and include in acceptance testing; coordinate with operator console team.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine Teleoperation module scope to explicitly include: (1) vehicle-side real-time processing (sensor acquisition, command processing) via coordination with Vehicle module; (2) network transport optimization and QoS coordination via Network Link interface; (3) operator-side command buffering and latency feedback; (4) latency monitoring, health reporting, and failover logic. Establish clear latency budget allocation and SLAs with Vehicle and Network Link modules.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid edge compute + network QoS + monitoring) directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 (end-to-end latency ≤ 150 ms p95). The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide design and is decomposable into sub-DPs for vehicle processing, network transport, and monitoring. No uncovered functional gaps identified. Coupling is acceptable and managed via latency budgeting.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must coordinate with Vehicle for sensor/actuator latency. Risk of latency responsibility diffusion if Vehicle does not meet its latency budget. Mitigation: Establish Vehicle latency SLA and include in interface contract.

Teleoperation module depends on Network Link for QoS guarantees. Risk of latency diffusion if network infrastructure does not support agreed QoS. Mitigation: Establish network SLA and continuous monitoring.

Teleoperation module interfaces with Safety Services for latency health alerts. Risk of safety logic diffusion if Safety Services does not respond promptly to latency alerts. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert response SLA and include in Safety Services contract.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.9) for latency optimization. External dependencies (Vehicle, Network Link, Safety Services) are clearly defined via interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). Latency ownership is cohesive within Teleoperation; diffusion risks are manageable via SLAs and interface contracts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CR-NGHE-008 is verifiable via end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation (with Vehicle and Network Link mocks) and in integration. Latency metrics (p95, jitter, packet loss) are measurable and traceable to acceptance criteria. Operator acceptance testing is feasible.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Latency ownership is clearly allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) are allocated to specific components (vehicle processing, network transport, operator processing, monitoring). Latency budget allocation is explicit and verifiable. No ambiguity in ownership or responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Establish and document latency budget allocation: vehicle processing (<20 ms), network transport (<80 ms p95), operator processing (<30 ms), with margin for monitoring overhead and contingency.
Define SLAs for Vehicle module (sensor/actuator latency), Network Link (transport latency, QoS guarantees), and Safety Services (latency alert response time).
Specify latency monitoring mechanism: hardware timestamping, packet-level tracking, end-to-end measurement. Define latency health thresholds and failover logic.
Include latency acceptance testing in verification plan: measure p95 latency under representative network conditions, validate operator acceptance, document baseline and degradation scenarios.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is a high-level requirement that spans multiple architectural layers (vehicle processing, network transport, operator interface, safety monitoring). The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and Teleoperation is the correct module owner. However, the requirement is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs to guide detailed design. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) address specific latency contributors and must be refined further to establish design parameters and implementation modules for vehicle processing, network QoS, operator buffering, and monitoring.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Acquire sensor data and prepare telemetry packets with minimal acquisition latency (target: <20 ms)
FR-NGHE-008.2: Transmit telemetry and tasking commands over network link with bounded transit latency (target: <80 ms p95 under agreed conditions)
FR-NGHE-008.3: Process remote operator commands and generate actuator control signals with minimal processing latency (target: <30 ms)
FR-NGHE-008.4: Monitor and report latency health; trigger safety alerts if latency exceeds safe thresholds

assumptions: Network infrastructure supports agreed QoS profiles (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss).
Vehicle compute can sustain real-time processing without thermal or power constraints.
Operator-side command buffering does not introduce unacceptable control lag.
Latency monitoring overhead is negligible (<5 ms).
End-to-end latency is measured from sensor acquisition to actuator response, including all processing and transmission delays.
p95 latency is measured over representative operational scenarios (1000+ control cycles).
Agreed network conditions are defined and documented (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles).
open_risks: Network infrastructure may not support agreed QoS profiles in all operational areas. Mitigation: Establish network SLA and continuous monitoring; implement fallback modes if QoS degrades.
Vehicle compute may saturate under high sensor data rates. Mitigation: Implement adaptive sensor sampling and data compression; validate compute capacity under peak load.

Operator-side latency (command processing, display refresh) may exceed budget if operator console is not optimized. Mitigation: Coordinate with operator console team; include operator-side latency in acceptance testing.
Latency monitoring may introduce overhead or false positives. Mitigation: Use hardware timestamping; validate monitoring accuracy in lab and field testing.
Safety alert response time may be insufficient if Safety Services is overloaded. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert response SLA; implement priority queuing in Safety Services.
evidence_needed: Network SLA documentation (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles, QoS guarantees).
Vehicle compute capacity analysis (sensor data rates, processing latency, thermal/power constraints).
Latency budget allocation and justification (vehicle processing, network transport, operator processing, monitoring).
Latency monitoring design and validation (hardware timestamping, packet-level tracking, measurement accuracy).
Operator acceptance testing results (p95 latency under representative scenarios, operator feedback on responsiveness).
Failover and degradation testing (latency behavior under network degradation, safety alert response time).
Integration testing results (end-to-end latency measurement, cross-module coordination, SLA compliance).
